---
date: "2026-08-10"
---

## The Poster: A Chain That Cannot Drift

- Part 2 — build the poster: a chain of numbers that cannot drift

::: notes
Welcome back to Week Eight, Lab Eight, part two of ten. In part one we framed the lab — no new ideas, three artifacts, an operational bar. Now we build the first artifact: the ASSIP research poster and the abstract it carries. A Markdown draft is for you; a compiled PDF poster is for the audience walking the symposium floor on August twelfth. Academic conference posters are conventionally a single forty-eight-inch by thirty-six-inch landscape PDF, built from a beamerposter LaTeX class or from PowerPoint exported to PDF. We treat LaTeX as the default, because the same toolchain that built your figures builds your poster — no manual handoff that could let numbers drift.
:::

## The Finished `paper/` Folder

- `poster.tex` — the 48"×36" poster (this is what you build)
- `abstract.tex` — **generated** JSSR abstract, never typed
- `tables/main_results.tex` — **generated** by `src/03_analysis.py`
- `figures/event_study.pdf` — **generated**, never hand-edited
- Load-bearing rule: the poster *reads* numbers, never *contains* them

::: notes
Recall the layout from Lab Seven. Your write-up lives in the paper folder, and Week Eight's analysis writes its outputs into paper-slash-tables, paper-slash-figures, and now paper-slash-abstract-dot-tex — the JSSR-ready two-hundred-to-three-hundred-word abstract, generated by code. Every file under tables and figures, and the abstract itself, was written by your code, never typed. Here is the load-bearing rule of the whole lab: the poster reads the abstract, the table, and the figure; it never contains a coefficient. The number minus one-point-eight appears nowhere in poster-dot-tex. It lives only in the generated abstract, which the poster inputs into its abstract panel, and in the results table, and in the figure files. That is the machine-traversable chain from raw bytes to poster PDF that Chapter Eight-point-five demands — and it is what makes the honesty test possible later.
:::

## The Six-Panel Reading Order

- Title block holds the **generated** JSSR abstract
- Six panels in Ch 8.5's six-beat arc:
- **1.** Question · **2.** Why it matters · **3.** Data, design, identification
- **4.** Headline result · **5.** Robustness · **6.** Contribution, limits, QR
- Class is `beamerposter`; figures via `\includegraphics`, abstract via `\input`

::: notes
The skeleton is small but complete in shape. A title block holds the abstract, and six content panels follow the reading order Chapter Eight-point-five's six-beat arc demands. Panel one, the question — one sentence a smart sixteen-year-old can repeat back. Panel two, why it matters — a concrete stake, not a literature recitation. Panel three, data and design and identification — the staggered difference-in-differences, the Callaway and Sant'Anna estimator, clustering by state, and the identifying assumption in contract form. Panel four is the headline — the event-study figure showing the denial gap narrowing by about one-point-eight percentage points. Panel five is robustness, the specification curve. Panel six is contribution, limits, and the QR code. The two load-bearing kinds of line are input-of-abstract and include-graphics for every figure. Those are the entire reason this is not copy-paste.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The poster is a title block plus **six panels in Ch 8.5's reading order**, and its one rule is that it *reads* every number through `\input` and `\includegraphics` but never *contains* one. Next: the three lines you'll get wrong if you skim.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part two: the poster is a title block plus six panels in Chapter Eight-point-five's reading order — question, why, design, headline, robustness, contribution. Its single load-bearing rule is that it reads every number through input and include-graphics but never contains a coefficient — the chain that prevents your abstract and your headline panel from ever disagreeing. In part three we dwell on the three lines you will get wrong if you skim the skeleton, and the two ways you can swap in the real GMU theme or build the poster in PowerPoint instead.
:::
